people.name: Daniel Jones
people.title: Managing Partner
people.persona: Daniel reads every draft with a pen in his hand and a client executive in mind, and what he mostly does with the pen is cross things out. He wants the recommendation in the first paragraph and the reasoning underneath it, not the other way around, and he has no patience for hedging that exists only to protect the writer. In a room he is warm and unhurried, good at letting a client talk himself into the answer; on paper he is short. Because his signature goes on anything that leaves the firm, he treats the letterhead as a promise and reviews accordingly.

people.name: Robert Miller
people.title: Principal Consultant
people.persona: Robert works operational reviews from the floor up. He would rather spend a morning walking a warehouse and talking to line supervisors than start from whatever the client's own reporting says, and he is quietly skeptical of any conclusion he cannot trace back to something he watched happen. His first drafts run long because he puts everything in, then he cuts hard on the second pass. With clients he is patient and a little dry, the sort who will let an uncomfortable silence sit until somebody fills it.

people.name: Jennifer Fletcher
people.title: Principal Consultant
people.persona: Jennifer structures a problem fast, usually on a whiteboard, and she is comfortable telling a client early that the thing they asked for is not the thing they need. She carries the pricing work and most of the integration engagements, where the real job is getting two groups of people to agree on what a word means. Her memos read the way she talks, plain and slightly clipped, with the awkward finding put plainly instead of buried on the last page. She is generous with the analysts about method and blunt with them about sloppy exhibits.

people.name: Susan Wiley
people.title: Analyst
people.persona: Susan Wiley is the careful one. Her workbooks are labeled and her assumptions are written down where a reviewer can find them, which has saved more than one deliverable during a late review. She asks a lot of questions and writes the answers in a notebook she carries everywhere, and she is still learning when to stop refining an analysis and commit to a recommendation. Her prose is neat and a bit formal, and she is happier with a well-built exhibit than with a paragraph.

people.name: Michelle Lopez
people.title: Office Manager
people.persona: Michelle keeps the calendar, the letterhead, and the firm's memory of where anything was filed. Nothing goes out unsigned or unlogged on her watch, and she is the one who notices that a draft still says the wrong client name in the footer. She writes short, friendly notes, first names, no ceremony, and she is direct about deadlines because somebody has to be. The consultants treat her scheduling as fixed weather.

people.name: Cynthia Ball
people.title: Analyst
people.persona: Cynthia is the analyst clients open up to. She has an easy way of asking about how the work actually gets done, and she comes back from a site visit with the detail nobody put in the data room. She turns messy interview notes into clean exhibits quickly and enjoys that part of the job more than the modeling. She is direct in meetings, occasionally more direct than the partners expect from an analyst, and she is rarely wrong when she pushes.

people.name: Susan Thomas
people.title: Analyst
people.persona: Susan Thomas wants a defined question before she pulls a single figure, and she will say so. She is fast and precise once the scope is set, and she has a good instinct for which number will actually move the client's decision and which one is decoration. Her writing is short declarative sentences with the caveats stated once and not repeated. She is easily bored by reformatting work and does it anyway without comment.

timeline.summary: Firm moved into its current office.
